author_headline: Associate Professor at The Wharton School. Author of Co-Intelligence
post_text:
I had Fable build another thing I always wanted, a full procedural fantasy kingdom generator with economics, trade routes, population growth, wars, lineages, disasters, geography, and occasional dragons. First, I worked with it on a plan, then it made it happen.

You can play it here: https://lnkd.in/eXx83n4t

11 page prompt: https://lnkd.in/epZRiqeD

variants.comment:
The 11-page prompt is the part that caught my attention most — sharing the actual design document alongside the playable result is a rare move.

Curious how Fable handles prompt drift over time: once the world starts generating its own history (wars, lineages, disasters accumulating), does the 11-page prompt stay the authoritative source of truth for what the simulation should be, or does the generated state gradually take over as the real spec? Wondering if you've tried feeding the evolved world state back into a revised prompt, or whether that loop even makes sense in Fable's model.
variants.rationale: The 11-page prompt is the real artifact and raises a genuine open question about whether the prompt or the generated world state becomes the source of truth as simulation history accumulates.

variants.comment:
The "worked on a plan first, then it made it happen" framing is the interesting part here — most people skip straight to prompting for output and skip the planning pass entirely.

What's the failure mode you'd worry about as the simulation runs longer? My read is that the 11-page prompt embeds assumptions about how economies and lineages interact that probably hold for the first few generations but could drift as edge cases compound. Does Fable have a way to surface when the generated world state starts contradicting the design intent baked into the prompt — or is that a feature, not a bug?
variants.rationale: The plan-first then execute workflow is slightly under-examined; pushing on the failure mode when simulation state diverges from prompt assumptions pulls the conversation into sharper territory.

variants.comment:
Sharing the 11-page prompt alongside the playable demo is the right call — the prompt is the actual design artifact, the generated world is just one instantiation of it.

This is worth sitting with: most people will spend 20 minutes playing the kingdom generator and zero minutes reading the prompt. But the prompt is what encodes the economics model, the disaster logic, the lineage rules. The world is disposable; the prompt is what you'd version-control. There's something here about where authorship lives when the output is procedurally generated — the author of the simulation is whoever wrote the 11 pages, not whoever clicked "generate."
variants.rationale: Reframes the post's center of gravity from the playable world to the prompt-as-design-document, arguing the prompt is the persistent artifact and the generated world is disposable.

slack_summary: Used Fable to build a playable procedural fantasy kingdom generator, and shared both the game and the full 11-page prompt behind it.

author_headline: I help teams use AI coding tools without losing control of requirements, tests, architecture and quality | BDD, TDD & Test Automation Expert | Author of BDD in Action
post_text:
Honest question for the BDD crowd: do we still need Gherkin? Spec-driven development needs executable specifications, and Gherkin (Given/When/Then) has been the default way to write them for years.
But I keep wondering whether it's still earning its place.
On the one hand, non-developers can read it, it doubles as living documentation, and the format forces clear thinking about preconditions and outcomes.
But on the other hand: it's a layer on top of your step definitions, and plenty of teams write Gherkin no business stakeholder ever reads. If your acceptance tests are written to be readable anyway, you get the living documentation without the extra layer.
AI complicates things a bit more. AI can turn examples into Gherkin, but it's still pretty rubbish at writing _good_ Gherkin. It's usually capable, though, of turning examples straight into clean, readable acceptance tests. So is the Gherkin step adding understanding, or just translation overhead?
I don't have a settled answer. Where do you land: does Gherkin still pull its weight, or has readable, code-based living documentation made it optional?

variants.comment:
The framing of Gherkin as a readability layer is the right lens — but I'd push back on the conclusion it leads to.

The case against Gherkin usually runs: stakeholders don't read it anyway, so it's just ceremony on top of step definitions. Fair. But I think that argument confuses the artifact with the process. Gherkin's real value was never the file — it was forcing a structured conversation about preconditions and outcomes before anyone touched code. That conversation is what produces the examples. If AI now turns those examples straight into tests, fine, but someone still has to have the conversation that generates the examples in the first place. Gherkin made that mandatory; dropping it doesn't make the conversation happen automatically.

Where I'm genuinely uncertain: does AI change who needs to be in that conversation? If a product manager can now describe an example in plain prose and an AI turns it into a working test, Gherkin starts to look like a format tax paid by engineers for a collaboration problem that could be solved differently. Curious whether your experience with teams using AI coding tools has shifted where you see that conversation breaking down — at the example-gathering stage, or later?
variants.rationale: John leaves room for disagreement; Petro's True BDD thesis (conversation over artifact) gives a genuine counter-position to the readability-layer framing without being hostile.

variants.comment:
The point about AI generating clean, readable acceptance tests directly from examples is the one that keeps nagging at me too.

But here's what I keep coming back to: the bottleneck was never the format. It was the quality of the examples themselves. If a team writes vague examples, AI produces clean tests for vague behavior — and they pass, because the system does exactly what the bad example described. Gherkin's structured syntax at least created friction at the source, forcing people to be specific about "given this exact state" rather than "given the user is logged in somehow." Does readable, code-based living documentation have an equivalent forcing function, or does it just make it easier to be confidently wrong?
variants.rationale: The post debates the format around examples but assumes example quality is a given; this question pulls that blindspot into focus.

variants.strategy_label: Ask a clarifying question rooted in the topic
variants.comment:
The observation that plenty of teams write Gherkin no business stakeholder ever reads is the one that deserves more scrutiny — because it might be diagnosing the wrong problem.

When stakeholders don't read Gherkin, is it because the format is wrong, or because the Gherkin was never actually shown to them — never used as the conversation artifact it was designed to be, and instead written post-hoc by developers as a test-scripting language dressed up in Given/When/Then? In that case, replacing Gherkin with readable code-based tests doesn't solve the underlying collaboration gap; it just removes one more reason to have the conversation. Curious whether the teams you're seeing ditch Gherkin successfully had already solved that collaboration problem by other means, or whether they're just skipping the part where product and engineering align on behavior before writing anything.
variants.rationale: The 'stakeholders don't read it' claim has an unexamined variable — was Gherkin used as a collaboration tool at all? Targets that gap and invites John's field observation.

slack_summary: Asks the BDD community whether Gherkin (Given/When/Then) still earns its place now that AI can turn examples straight into clean, readable acceptance tests.

author_headline: Senior Technology Leader | Technology at the Service of the Business | Experienced in leading and scaling large technology organizations | Startup Advisor | Adjunct lecturer at the University of Geneva
post_text:
AI is making development faster.

So why are many teams not seeing the productivity multiplier they expected?
My current hypothesis: because development (specifications, architecture, coding, testing) was not the only bottleneck. It was just hiding the others.

As AI accelerates local execution, the real constraints become much more visible: dependencies between teams, handoffs between roles, unclear product intent, enterprise platform queues, late validation, and component-based task splitting.

In other words, AI does not automatically improve flow. It accelerates the work until the work hits the organizational wall.

I wrote a longer article on this through the lens of Spec Driven Development and Agentic PDLC : "L'enfer, c'est les autres" : Why AI Exposes Dependency Management as the Real Bottleneck in Software Delivery

Curious where others are seeing the bottleneck move: coding, requirements, dependencies, platform self-service, or organizational design?

variants.comment:
Agreed that coding was hiding the real constraints — the moment the "easy" part gets fast, everything downstream becomes visible.

Where I'd push back slightly: "unclear product intent" isn't just an organizational problem — it's a spec problem that predates the handoff. When the spec isn't the source of truth, intent leaks at every translation step: PM to engineer, engineer to AI, AI to code. You get faster execution of the wrong thing. So in my experience the bottleneck isn't just dependencies between teams, it's that the artifact carrying intent (the spec) degrades before it reaches anyone who could act on it.

Which makes me curious about your Agentic PDLC framing specifically: when you say spec-driven development, do you mean spec-as-source (the spec is the artifact you edit, code gets derived) or spec-as-kickoff (the spec starts the work, then code takes over)? Because those two have very different answers to the dependency problem.
variants.rationale: Sharpens Steve's 'org bottleneck' claim by reframing unclear product intent as a spec-degradation problem upstream of any handoff — directly on Petro's public thesis.

variants.comment:
The "organizational wall" framing is exactly right — AI doesn't improve flow, it makes the ceiling visible faster.

Here's the question that sits in the back of my head though: if agentic PDLC is the proposed fix — autonomous agents spanning the full delivery lifecycle — doesn't that require those same org dependencies to be machine-readable? An agent hitting a handoff between teams still needs to know who to ask, what to ask for, and when the answer is good enough. That's not a tooling problem, it's a governance problem. And governance tends to move at the speed of the slowest stakeholder, not the fastest model. So what does "agentic PDLC" actually mean when the process it's automating is itself political?
variants.rationale: Pulls the conversation into its sharpest unexamined tension — agentic PDLC solving org bottlenecks requires those bottlenecks to be machine-readable, which they are not.

variants.comment:
The "development was hiding the other bottlenecks" observation is the clearest way I've seen this put.

You list five candidates for where the bottleneck moves: coding, requirements, dependencies, platform self-service, organizational design. Given that you work across both enterprise orgs and startups as an advisor — do you see the bottleneck landing in different places depending on company size? My intuition is that in startups the constraint shifts to requirements clarity almost immediately, while in enterprise it's the dependency/platform queues that dominate. Curious whether that maps to what you're seeing or whether organizational design cuts across both.
variants.rationale: Exploits Steve's dual position as enterprise tech leader and startup advisor to ask a question requiring real comparative experience.

slack_summary: Argues AI speeds up coding but doesn't improve flow — it just exposes the real bottlenecks (team dependencies, handoffs, unclear intent) as the organizational wall.

author_headline: Technology Director
post_text:
From a thing I'm writing (news soon):

"When code becomes cheaper, you get a lot more of it. However, code that's easier to produce isn't automatically easier to guide to a high-quality outcome. To harness agentic coding at scale, you have to encode your intent so that it's available to coding agents.

That's where spec-driven development (SDD) comes in. A specification or spec is a harness that describes qualities that the solution must have. Spec-driven development is a software engineering approach where a spec is the primary artifact from which the implementation flows. Effective spec-driven development is about channeling intent into the code-generation process, suggesting possibilities, and constraining solutions.

Early adopters of spec-driven development with coding agents focused on unstructured Markdown as a way to build specs, but natural language is too imprecise to reliably specify systems at scale on its own. To effectively capture your expectations, your spec should make use of a variety of formats. Spec-driven development includes any kind of artefact that can guide and constrain code generation — not only Markdown, but contracts, models, examples, and tests."

variants.comment:
The point about intent encoding being the real bottleneck is one I keep running into — more code volume just makes the guidance problem more visible, not more tractable.

One thing your framing leaves open: what validates the spec itself? You describe the spec as the harness that constrains code generation, but in practice I've found that the same LLM that generates the code often generated (or refined) the spec too. When both artifacts come from the same model, passing tests aren't strong evidence that the intent was captured correctly — they're evidence that the model's interpretation of the spec was self-consistent. Curious whether you see a practical answer to that, or whether this is one of the things the piece addresses.
variants.rationale: Opens a precise, unresolved question in Chris's framing (LLM-generated spec validated by LLM-generated tests) without contradicting his thesis.

variants.comment:
The multi-format point lands well — contracts, models, examples, and tests each carry intent that prose can't.

The harder question underneath it: what happens to the spec once the code exists? Most of the tools and practices I've seen treat the spec as the primary artifact at project start, but the code quietly becomes the source of truth the moment an agent (or a human) edits it directly. The spec becomes aspirational documentation rather than a constraint. So the real design challenge might not be "how do we write better specs" but "how do we make editing the code directly more expensive than editing the spec." Does your piece go there?
variants.rationale: Sharpens a blindspot — spec primacy at generation time vs. at iteration time — with a reframe that's contrarian but not hostile.

variants.comment:
Agreed on the Markdown-only path — natural language gives agents too much interpretive freedom, and at scale that freedom accumulates into drift.

One tension I'd push on: the framing here is that the spec is the artifact from which implementation flows, which is true at generation time. But what's the failure mode at iteration time? When a stakeholder asks for a change and the developer edits the code directly — or an agent does — the spec stops being the primary artifact without anyone deciding that. The interesting spec-driven designs I've seen have to answer the question of whether editing the code is even permitted, or whether all changes must come through the spec. That boundary is where "spec-driven" separates into quite different things in practice.
variants.rationale: Introduces the spec-anchored vs. spec-as-source divide as a counter-framing, adding a specific boundary the post doesn't draw.

slack_summary: Previews a piece on spec-driven development: to scale agentic coding you must encode intent as the primary artifact, using not just Markdown but contracts, models, examples, and tests.

author_headline: Developer - MinimumCD.org co-author, SADMF.com certified
post_text:
This repository has never been reviewed by me. It's 100% maintained by robots. It was built from "this is what I need" and "this isn't working as expected. Here's what good looks like." while using my code review and test design review skills.
https://lnkd.in/gB9Mpfqb
Here's the experiment:
Some people claim building things this way will result in  unmaintInable garbage and growing tech debt.
Code review it and demonstrate the claim.

variants.comment:
The challenge is a good one — code review is exactly the right pressure test here, not vibes-based "it feels messy."

The variable I'd add though: the quality of human feedback you're providing isn't free. "Here's what good looks like" presupposes someone who already knows what good looks like — you're essentially running a spec-as-feedback loop with a strong senior reviewer in the chair. The question that would really break or validate the experiment is: what does the maintainability curve look like if the human in the loop is a mid-level engineer who's learned to accept whatever AI produces? Because then you're not testing "can robots maintain a codebase" — you're testing "can a strong human steer robots to maintain a codebase," which is a narrower and more honest claim.
variants.rationale: Surfaces the hidden variable (expert human in the loop) that the experiment conflates with 'robots maintaining code,' pushing Bryan toward a sharper claim boundary.

variants.comment:
The experiment is fair, and I think the people predicting garbage will mostly be wrong — for the wrong reasons.

My counter to the "growing tech debt" crowd isn't about code quality, it's about determinism over time. If two robots build from the same feedback history, do they produce consistent behaviour? Because if the answer is "kind of," then the code is still the source of truth, and someone still has to review everything it produces — which means the human is still the bottleneck. The maintainability question and the consistency question look the same on day one and diverge badly by month six. Curious whether you've seen that show up yet in this repo.
variants.rationale: Brings Petro's thesis (determinism over time is the real test, not day-one code quality) into the debate, adding a concrete dimension beyond what the challengers measure.

variants.comment:
Setting up a public code review as the burden-of-proof mechanism is the right move — claims about unmaintainability tend to evaporate when you have to show the specific line where it breaks down.

One thing I'm curious about: when you say you're using your "code review and test design review skills" as the steering mechanism — how explicit is that feedback? Are you writing it as structured acceptance criteria, or is it more conversational? Asking because the feedback format seems like it could be the actual variable that determines whether this stays coherent as the codebase grows.
variants.rationale: Targets the unexamined input format (structured vs. conversational feedback) as the variable Bryan hasn't addressed.

slack_summary: Shares a repo he's never reviewed, 100% maintained by AI agents, and challenges skeptics to code-review it and prove the 'unmaintainable garbage / tech debt' claim.

post_text:
AI can generate skills in seconds.
The difficult part is knowing whether those changes are actually ready to merge.
Tessl introduces the new Tessl Review, a major upgrade that helps teams review AI-generated skill changes against the standards that matter to their organisation.
Every team has a different idea of what "good" looks like. Some care about architecture patterns, others prioritise security, coding conventions, domain-specific requirements, or internal best practices. A single default score can't capture all of that.
With custom review rubrics, you can define your own review criteria and evaluate skills against your team's standards, not somebody else's.
The new review experience also brings more accurate agentic reviews, a shared history of review runs, and tessl review fix to help improve skills that don't meet the quality bar.
Because faster software delivery shouldn't come at the cost of quality.
Read the blog to learn how Tessl Review helps teams build, review, and improve skills with confidence.
https://lnkd.in/gnxgtHny

variants.comment:
The rubrics point is the real unlock here — a "good" score meaning something different for a fintech team vs a data pipeline team vs an OSS lib is exactly the right problem to name.

Curious how the rubric evaluation works under the hood: does Tessl Review run the rubric as an LLM judge against the skill diff, or is there something more deterministic involved? The failure mode I'd worry about is rubric drift — the LLM grades the same change differently on Monday vs Friday, which means your quality bar shifts without anyone noticing. Has that come up in testing?
variants.rationale: Tessl's post leaves the rubric evaluation mechanism unaddressed — an LLM-judge implementation has a well-known non-determinism problem that directly undercuts the quality-gate promise, making this the sharpest specific question to ask.

variants.comment:
Custom review rubrics fix a real thing — org-specific standards can't be encoded in a generic score, and teams waste time arguing about whether AI output meets "their" bar rather than a shared one.

The thing I keep turning over: if the spec is the source of truth, shouldn't the spec itself be the rubric? The review question then becomes "does the generated skill match the spec" — deterministic, not LLM-judged. Custom rubrics are a good answer for the world where code stays the source of truth. But if that assumption ever shifts, the rubric layer dissolves. Is the Tessl bet that code stays the source of truth indefinitely, or is the rubric a bridge while model reliability catches up?
variants.rationale: Pulls the conversation into Petro's core thesis territory (spec as source of truth) without pitching TrueBDD by name — frames it as a genuine strategic question about Tessl's long-term architecture bet.

variants.comment:
The observation that faster delivery shouldn't come at the cost of quality is one worth defending — most "ship fast, fix later" arguments quietly assume the cost of "later" is manageable, which is usually wrong.

Where I'd push back a little: custom rubrics still assume someone needs to evaluate the generated code against those rubrics. That keeps review on the critical path — the rubric makes it smarter but not necessarily faster. The alternative bet is to move the source of truth to the spec and reduce the review surface rather than improve it: if the spec is what's reviewed and the code is derived from it, you're not grading the output, you're grading the input. Both are real bets. Curious whether Tessl sees rubrics as the long-term answer or a bridge while underlying model reliability closes the gap.
variants.rationale: Engages Tessl's implicit assumption (code stays the source of truth, therefore review stays on the critical path) from Petro's documented contrarian position, without dismissing their work or naming TrueBDD as a competitor.

slack_summary: Tessl launches "Tessl Review" — custom rubrics that let teams grade AI-generated code changes against their own org-specific standards instead of one generic score.

author_headline: Co-founder @ Moderne | Java Champion | founder OpenRewrite - OSS automated refactoring
post_text:
Ouch. That's cold. 6 months after the "nobody looks at the IDE anymore" trend, one missing bang and it's like CC doesn't even know that IntelliJ exists, haha.
The reality is though, I'm looking more and more at the IDE. At a minimum, I'm finding I need to cut absurdly verbose comments everywhere. The comment pattern that I feel is particularly detrimental is the one that basically records what the code USED to do, or did in some iteration towards the final design, not what it CURRENTLY does. So there is a token record of discarded ideas that jointly serve as a kind of gravity well sucking a model back to those discarded ideas.
Strangely the better the model gets, the more convoluted the code is getting. A lot of folks are saying it doesn't matter, that engineers should move up the stack and become product managers and designers and even that models should stop writing in human-centric programming languages altogether.
I went through a phase where I used Zed just because it was faster to ! start from a CC session. It's faster though because it's far less capable. So I've boomeranged a bit. Slow is smooth, smooth is fast.

variants.comment:
The "gravity well of discarded ideas" is the most precise framing of this problem I've seen — stale comments aren't just noise, they're literally attractor states that pull the next generation toward decisions that were already rejected.

Curious what you've landed on as the actual remedy. Is it a deletion discipline (if a comment describes what code used to do, it gets cut immediately), or are you experimenting with a structured format that makes the model-facing intent explicit rather than archaeological?
variants.rationale: Jonathan identified a sharp mechanism; asking what fix he's found in practice extends his thought without flattering.

variants.comment:
The "better the model, the more convoluted the code" observation is real and I think under-examined. The model doesn't just accept the context you give it — it tries to be consistent with it, so a codebase full of half-baked iterations becomes a very persuasive prior.

The part I'd push on: is pruning comments in the IDE the right intervention, or is it a symptom that we're still treating code as the ground truth the model reasons from? If the spec were the source of truth and code were genuinely disposable — regenerated from spec rather than incrementally patched — the comment archaeology problem mostly disappears. The question is whether the "slow is smooth" answer is "better IDE hygiene" or "wrong layer altogether."
variants.rationale: Pushes on whether IDE pruning solves the root cause or patches a symptom, opening the spec-as-source-of-truth angle without pitching TrueBDD.

variants.comment:
The boomerang back to the IDE makes sense — at some point you need eyes on what's actually in the file, not just what the model tells you is there.

Where I'd push back slightly: the "cut verbose comments" workflow feels like a patch on a deeper issue. Code is being used as the model's memory — and commented-out iteration history is just the most visible symptom. The underlying problem is that the model's context is the code, so everything that accumulates in the code (including ghost comments) becomes its worldview. I'm skeptical the solution scales past "discipline." At some point the pressure is to stop treating code as the source of truth at all — let the spec carry that weight and treat the code as something you can delete and regenerate. Whether that's practical today is a different question, but the trend Jonathan describes seems to push in that direction whether we intend it to or not.
variants.rationale: Frames Petro's core thesis (code is disposable, spec is source of truth) as a genuine counter-position to comment hygiene as the fix, without naming TrueBDD.

slack_summary: Reflects that he's back to looking at the IDE more, mainly to cut AI-generated stale comments that record what code used to do and pull the model back toward discarded ideas.

author_headline: Software Engineer. Physicist. Mathematician.
post_text:
Connect
Last week I wrote boids in Erlang with ASCII rendering in the terminal. Several people asked: why not real graphics? Fair. So I split the project in two - and put my two worlds into one codebase. The server is an Erlang node. Every boid is a separate supervised process with its own state. The simulation is nothing but messages between processes - no shared memory exists anywhere. The client is a macOS app: Swift + Metal, instanced rendering, one triangle per boid rotated by its heading in the vertex shader.
Between them: TCP and a tiny binary protocol. And here is my favorite detail. Each boid on the wire is {x, y, vx, vy} as four little-endian float32 - which is byte-for-byte the memory layout of float4 in Metal Shading Language. The client receives a frame and does a single memcpy into the GPU instance buffer. The wire format IS the GPU format. No parsing, no conversion, nothing.
The demo is still my favorite party trick. Press K: the app sends one byte, the server runs exit(Pid, kill) on a random boid process. The supervisor restarts it within microseconds - on screen the boid just teleports to a new position. The flock never notices. Population: unchanged. That last part confused me for a moment during testing: kill a boid, spawn a boid, and the count goes UP by one. Of course it does. In OTP, a crash is not a removal. A permanent child cannot be killed out of existence - only supervisor:terminate_child/2 removes it administratively. "Killed" and "dismissed" are different operations, and the runtime enforces the difference. Two programs, two paradigms: the GPU side optimized for throughput, the BEAM side optimized for survival, meeting over a 16-byte-per-boid protocol. Zero external dependencies on either side.

variants.comment: The framing of "paradox" is doing a lot of work here. The question I'd push on: when hiring managers say senior talent "isn't available," are they talking about inbound pipeline, referral reach, or cold outreach conversion? Because those three channels have very different explanations for why they're broken right now, and treating them as one thing makes the diagnosis harder.
variants.rationale: Splits vague "unavailable" into three concrete hiring channels to force a sharper diagnosis.

variants.comment:
The tension looks paradoxical until you separate two things: "not available on the open market" vs. "not available to be recruited." Senior engineers with strong track records rarely post their CVs — they get moved by relationships, referrals, and reputation. If a hiring manager's primary motion is posting a JD and filtering applications, they will genuinely not find that talent.

The senior engineer, meanwhile, is sending applications into an ATS that was calibrated for a different hiring volume and getting zero signal back. Both are experiencing real friction; they're just on different tracks entirely.
variants.rationale: Resolves the paradox by naming the open-market vs. relationship-recruited split.

variants.comment: The setup is sharp. One thing I'd want to know: are the "experienced professionals" who can't get responses applying to roles at companies that say they can't find senior engineers — or is it two completely different segments of the market talking past each other? The answer changes what's actually being diagnosed here.
variants.rationale: Tests whether the paradox is one data set or two separate market segments.

author_headline: AI Agent Engineer for QA and Dev teams, Building Endorphin AI and Robots
post_text:
You know what a great prompt looks like — and you never write one.
Not because you can't. You've read every guide. You could list the parts in your sleep: role, context, constraints, output format, the edge cases, an example or two.
Then you open Claude and type six words. No stack, no priorities, no output shape. It works well enough, so you re-roll it three times and call that "iterating."
That's the bad prompt. It's the one every senior engineer actually types — because the good one costs 90 seconds of specification you'll never spend at 9am.
The good prompt is a different species. It names the stack. It ranks what matters. It pins the output format so Claude stops guessing. It reads like a spec, because it is one. You know exactly how to write it. You just won't.
So stop writing prompts.
I type one line — /prompt-expert, then what I want — and the skill interviews me. Stack? Risks? Severity buckets? Output shape? The four questions I'm too lazy to answer unprompted. Then it hands back the good prompt, fully specified, because it asked instead of assumed.
The move was never "write a better prompt." It's refusing to write the prompt at all, and building an agent that interviews you to do it.
And yes — you're using Claude to write the prompt you're about to hand Claude. That's not the bug. That's the whole point.

author_headline: Head of R&D at SoftwareMill, a division of VirtusLab; Software Engineer; Distributed Systems & Scala Expert; OSS Developer
post_text:
Should code reviews still be a separate stage in the software development process?
Code reviews used to be heavyweight: they required involving another human, which is an expensive and slow process.
But when agents review the code of other agents (or humans), that's no longer the case. It's trivial to run code reviews on-demand, multiple times, until all the problems are fixed.
Hence, can code reviews become just another quality gate in software development, alongside compilers, linters, and static analysis tools like Sonar?
That's definitely my experience. I always self-reviewed code before handing it over for further review, so the agentic review loop resembles that. But now, we can review using "fresh" agents or completely different models. So for me, code review used to be an end-of-the-line process, a final quality check. Now it's just a part of the iteration.
Which also brings the question: do we need specialized code review systems? Or is a refined prompt, or a lightweight CLI tool enough?
(As a side note: code reviews have always been close to my heart; one of our first (failed) startups was CodeBrag - a per-commit code-review tool. Some of the ideas were implemented on GitHub later, so, as always, we've been ahead of our time! ;) )

variants.comment:
The agentic review loop collapsing the "wait-for-a-human" bottleneck is real and the analogy to self-review before handoff lands well.

One thing I keep running into: reviewing and trusting the review are different problems. If the agent reviewing the diff was drawn from the same model family as the agent that wrote it, they share blind spots. You catch style issues, obvious logic errors, missing edge cases the training distribution covers — but the systematic failure modes of that model class slip through both passes. It's a bit like asking two students who memorized the same textbook to proofread each other's exam answers.

The question your post raises — specialized system vs. refined prompt — probably hinges on this. A prompt is enough if what you need is "catch what a different pass would catch." A specialized system starts making sense when you need to verify behaviour that the generating agent can't self-validate. What's your read on where the line actually is?
variants.rationale: Argues Petro's documented position (agent review shares model blind spots) in fresh framing without contradicting it.

variants.comment:
Framing code review as a quality gate rather than a handoff stage feels right — the heavyweight part was always the human scheduling, not the review itself.

The question that keeps me honest: what does an agent reviewer actually catch that a compiler or linter misses, and what does it systematically miss that a human reviewer catches? If those two lists mostly overlap with what static analysis already covers, the value of agentic review is convenience, not coverage. If they diverge sharply — especially on the "what it misses" side — then a refined prompt probably isn't enough, because you'd be optimizing for the wrong failure mode.

Have you found cases where the agent flagged something that would have slipped past Sonar but a human would have caught it differently — or the reverse?
variants.rationale: Pulls the conversation into coverage-vs-convenience — what agent review catches vs. systematically misses.

variants.comment:
The point about "fresh agents or completely different models" reviewing the same diff is the interesting part — cross-model validation is underused.

One variable your post leaves open: when the reviewing agent flags a problem and hands back to the generating agent, does the generating agent fix-then-return to the reviewer in the same session, or does it start a new context each time? Because if it's the same session, the reviewer's earlier framing leaks into the fix — you lose the "fresh eyes" benefit partway through the loop. If it's a new context each pass, the cost structure changes significantly. Curious whether that distinction showed up in your experience with CodeBrag's design, or whether the per-commit scope kept it contained.
variants.rationale: Surfaces context-leakage in the fix-then-reverify loop and ties it back to Adam's CodeBrag.

slack_summary: Argues that with agents reviewing agents, code review stops being a separate handoff stage and becomes just another quality gate in the loop.

author_headline: Co-founder @ Moderne | Java Champion | founder OpenRewrite - OSS automated refactoring
post_text:
Ouch. That's cold. 6 months after the "nobody looks at the IDE anymore" trend, one missing bang and it's like CC doesn't even know that IntelliJ exists, haha.
The reality is though, I'm looking more and more at the IDE. At a minimum, I'm finding I need to cut absurdly verbose comments everywhere. The comment pattern that I feel is particularly detrimental is the one that basically records what the code USED to do, or did in some iteration towards the final design, not what it CURRENTLY does. So there is a token record of discarded ideas that jointly serve as a kind of gravity well sucking a model back to those discarded ideas.
Strangely the better the model gets, the more convoluted the code is getting. A lot of folks are saying it doesn't matter, that engineers should move up the stack and become product managers and designers and even that models should stop writing in human-centric programming languages altogether.
I went through a phase where I used Zed just because it was faster to ! start from a CC session. It's faster though because it's far less capable. So I've boomeranged a bit. Slow is smooth, smooth is fast.

variants.comment:
The "gravity well of discarded ideas" framing is the most precise diagnosis of this problem I've read. A stale comment recording what the code used to do isn't noise — from the model's perspective, it's a signal, and a confident one.

Which makes me wonder: is the actual problem the capable IDE, or is it that inline comments are the closest thing the model has to a source of truth — and that source of truth happens to be contaminated? Cutting verbose comments is a patch. The failure mode you're describing sounds more like what happens when history lives inside the file instead of somewhere the model can read it before touching anything. What's your read on whether the problem survives if you give the model a clean spec document and strip comments entirely?
variants.rationale: Pulls the conversation past the IDE debate into the real unaddressed variable — whether moving the source of truth out of the file eliminates the gravity well entirely.

variants.comment:
The gravity well observation is right, and it points at something most people are still walking past. A comment recording what the code used to do isn't an inert artifact — the model treats it as documentation. So the pull isn't random noise; it's the model being faithful to the wrong source of truth.

I'd push back slightly on "slow is smooth" as the conclusion, though. Switching back to a capable IDE still leaves the same stale comment problem in the files — just with better tooling around it. The bet I'd make is that the real fix isn't IDE choice but source of truth: move the intent somewhere structured (a spec, a behavior doc, anything versioned outside the code) that the model reads first, so the inline comment stops being the most authoritative thing in context. Then the gravity well collapses because there's a stronger attractor. Curious whether you've experimented with that direction or if it feels like it trades one class of problem for another.
variants.rationale: Engages directly with Jonathan's 'slow is smooth' conclusion and redirects the diagnosis toward source-of-truth architecture, consistent with Petro's public thesis.

variants.comment:
The gravity well framing is sharp — and it's a different problem than "the model is bad at code." It's that the model is good at reading what's in front of it, and what's in front of it is a record of things you already decided not to do.

Curious how far you've pushed the comment-cutting: if you stripped inline comments almost entirely and instead gave the model a short external spec file describing current intent before every session, does the convoluted-code tendency go away, or does it find other things to anchor on? Asking because that seems like the natural experiment to isolate whether the gravity well is specifically a comment problem or a "model needs some prior context and will use whatever's available" problem.
variants.rationale: Surfaces the specific unexamined variable (external spec vs inline comments) with enough precision that answering requires Jonathan to have actually tried it.

slack_summary: Argues stale 'what the code used to do' comments act as a gravity well pulling AI models back to discarded designs; he's looking at the IDE more, not less.

author_headline: CEO @KronosX AI | Building the agentic data layer that makes Healthcare AI-Ready.
post_text:
Before AI can transform healthcare, it has to understand healthcare.
That's where most organizations get stuck.
The conversation today is dominated by bigger models, smarter agents, and new capabilities.
But after spending years working with health plans and building KronosX AI, I realized that biggest challenge isn't the AI, It's the data foundation underneath it.
In my latest newsletter, I share why AI-Ready Data is becoming the real competitive advantage, why infrastructure is quietly becoming the biggest AI investment, and what healthcare organizations need to get right before AI can create meaningful value.
If you're building, buying, or deploying AI in healthcare, I'd love to hear whether you've seen the same challenges.
Link to the newsletter below.

variants.comment:
We built a similar thing in banking before ChatGPT — a self-hosted GenAI trained on several million German-language loan and investment documents. The gap between our go-kart and GPT-4's Formula 1 car was enormous, and yet ours held up better for that specific task for years. The reason was exactly what you're describing: the model "understood" German banking documents because every training example, every validation loop, every failure mode was shaped around that domain's data structure.

Healthcare is a harder version of the same problem. Banking has messy data; healthcare has messy data plus regulatory constraints on how you can even touch it. I'd be curious what you've found to be the hardest part of the "AI-Ready" step — is it the schema normalization, the consent and access layer, or something you didn't see coming until you were deep in it?
variants.rationale: Petro's BankAI experience (documented 05.03.2026 post) is a direct analogue — domain-specific AI winning because of how the data was shaped, which is exactly Raj's thesis applied to healthcare.

variants.comment:
The framing "AI-Ready Data" resonates — the model is rarely the bottleneck, the data contract underneath it usually is.

One thing I'm curious about: when you say "AI-Ready," do you mean the data needs to be structured and labeled so a model can consume it, or something deeper — like a semantic layer that encodes healthcare-specific relationships (diagnoses, procedures, payer logic) so the AI doesn't have to hallucinate them from general pretraining? Those feel like two very different problems, and the second one is an order of magnitude harder to sell as infrastructure investment.
variants.rationale: The post uses 'AI-Ready Data' as a given without defining it — asking Raj to distinguish structural labeling from semantic encoding forces a more precise conversation.

variants.comment:
The infrastructure argument is solid, and I think you're right that it's underweighted in the current conversation.

The question I'd push on: data infrastructure in healthcare tends to get built once and then become organizational bedrock — which means the early movers don't just get a head start, they potentially lock in the ontology that every downstream AI application inherits. Is that a feature or a bug? Because if the ontology is wrong, or if it bakes in historical biases in how conditions are coded, "AI-Ready" might just mean "faster propagation of existing structural problems."
variants.rationale: The post celebrates early infrastructure investment but doesn't examine the risk of locking in a flawed ontology — surfacing this sharpens the conversation without being hostile.

slack_summary: Argues healthcare AI's real bottleneck isn't the models but the 'AI-Ready' data foundation underneath, which he sees becoming the biggest quiet AI investment.

author_headline: Founder @MS Innovation Hub | AI & Custom Software | We're Hiring Developers Passionate About AI
post_text:
I think the hardest part of building software has changed. 🤔
A few years ago, the biggest challenge was simply getting an idea built.
You needed developers, time, budget, and a lot of patience. Execution was the bottleneck.
Today, that's no longer true.
With AI, you can build prototypes, automate workflows, or even launch an MVP in days instead of months.
But after working with companies across manufacturing, logistics, and other industries, I've realized something.
Building the solution is no longer the difficult part.
Getting people to actually use it is.
I've seen technically great solutions fail because they didn't fit into the way people work. At the same time, I've seen relatively simple tools create huge value because they solved one annoying problem that employees faced every day.
That's why most of our conversations with clients don't start with AI.
They start with questions like:
👉 Where do people lose the most time?
👉 Which process frustrates employees the most?
👉 What information are people constantly searching for?
Only after we understand that do we start talking about technology.
AI has dramatically reduced the cost of building software.
It hasn't reduced the cost of understanding people.
And I think that's becoming the real competitive advantage.
The companies that win won't necessarily be the ones building the most AI.
They'll be the ones building AI that people actually want to use.
Have you seen this in your own company?
#ArtificialIntelligence #DigitalTransformation #BusinessTransformation #ProcessImprovement #Operations #Manufacturing #Leadership #MSiHub

variants.comment:
The shift from "execution is the bottleneck" to "adoption is the bottleneck" is something I keep watching play out across the teams we work with — and you've named it cleanly.

What I'm curious about: how do you run those discovery conversations in practice? The three questions you list (where do people lose time, what frustrates them, what are they always searching for) — are you doing structured process observation on-site, or is this mostly interviews? I ask because we've found that what people say in a conversation and what they actually do in front of a screen diverge pretty significantly, especially in manufacturing contexts where the workaround is so habitual nobody thinks to mention it.
variants.rationale: The post names the right questions to ask but doesn't say how — this pulls the conversation into the methodology gap that matters most for practitioners.

variants.comment:
"AI has dramatically reduced the cost of building software. It hasn't reduced the cost of understanding people." — that's the most precise sentence I've read on this topic in months.

One thing I'd push on though: if building got cheap and fast, there's a risk that the slow feedback loops that used to teach us what people actually needed get compressed too. When a prototype took six months, you had to talk to users for those six months. When it takes two weeks, you can skip that and just ship — and call the silence "validation." The bottleneck shifting to adoption might partly be a symptom of moving so fast that we're learning less along the way, not just building the wrong things.
variants.rationale: The post is optimistic about AI's role but misses that speed-to-build may compress the learning loops that built institutional knowledge about users — a sharper angle without being hostile.

variants.comment:
Agree that execution is no longer the bottleneck — and the framing around "understanding people" being the real moat holds up in our work too.

I'd add one complication though: not all companies that build AI people want to use will actually stay ahead, because "want to use" is a moving target. I've seen a pattern where teams ship something employees genuinely adopt, feel satisfied, then 18 months later the tool is shelf-ware again — not because it got worse, but because what people needed evolved and nobody had a process for catching that. Building AI people want to use is the entry requirement. Having a system that keeps learning what they want after deployment might be the actual competitive advantage.
variants.rationale: The post's conclusion is right but incomplete — the real differentiator is a continuous learning loop post-deployment, not just the initial fit.

slack_summary: Argues AI made building software cheap, so the hard part is now adoption — getting people to use it; understanding people is the real competitive advantage.

post_text:
If software quality works like proof-of-work, where more compute finds more bugs and more speed, then the best software is whatever had the most compute poured into it. So the real moat might not be cleverness. It might be gigawatts.
"So what you're suggesting," I said, "is there's a moat, and it's gigawatts."
It's the bet the frontier labs might be quietly making, that more problems get solved by brute force. He also said he doesn't know how much to believe it. Neither do I. But if it's right, the plumber can still write his own B-tree. He just can't afford the 400 gigawatt-hours that prove it's the best one.

variants.comment:
The gigawatt-as-moat framing feels right when you're building for breadth. The trouble is that domain specificity breaks the proof-of-work analogy.

We ran a GenAI system for German banking from 2018 to 2023 — pre-ChatGPT, team of 17, contracts worth maybe $30K. ChatGPT had billions of dollars of compute behind it and yet our narrowly-trained system outperformed it on that specific task until mid-2025. The B-tree the plumber writes for his actual pipes, with domain constraints baked in, may genuinely be better than the B-tree proved optimal by 400 gigawatt-hours on the wrong distribution.

The moat might be gigawatts or an unfair advantage in domain signal — and the second one is still affordable.
variants.rationale: Grounds disagreement in Petro BankAI story: narrow low-compute system beat a frontier model on the specific task

variants.comment:
The proof-of-work analogy is interesting precisely because it separates finding bugs from specifying what "correct" means.

Proof-of-work can find the optimal B-tree given a fitness function — but who writes the fitness function? If that definition stays the domain expert's job, then the compute moat applies to verification, not to specification. And if specification is where most production bugs actually live, the gigawatts may be buying the wrong thing.

Curious whether your framing assumes the fitness function is known — or whether that's the buried assumption.
variants.rationale: Shifts debate from how much compute to compute for what by surfacing the fitness-function problem

variants.comment:
The analogy holds if "quality" can be fully operationalized as a loss function the compute can optimize against.

My experience in production GenAI is that the hardest bugs are the ones where nobody wrote the test — not because testing is expensive, but because the correct behavior was never specified precisely enough to be testable. Does the proof-of-work model assume that specification problem is already solved, or is compute supposed to help there too?
variants.rationale: Surfaces the buried assumption that quality is fully measurable, grounded in production GenAI experience

slack_summary: Muses that software's real moat may be raw compute (gigawatts) rather than cleverness — if quality scales like proof-of-work.

author_headline: CTO at Checkr | Engineering leader across Fintech, Crypto & AI | 3x Founder (acquired) | Ex-Coinbase VP, Citadel
post_text:
I told 300+ engineers at Checkr, Inc. to stop looking for what's wrong during code reviews.
Most of the code we write is now generated by AI. There are more code changes than ever, and most of them are correct. After reviewing hundreds of clean changes, it's easy to approve the next one without reading it carefully.
People aren't being lazy. When nothing goes wrong for a long time, you naturally stop expecting problems.
It reminds me of Air France Flight 447.
The pilots had relied on autopilot for so long that, when it suddenly failed, they weren't ready to take over. The plane could have been saved, but it wasn't.
The same thing happens with AI.
When automation works well for long enough, people stop paying close attention.
So we changed the review process.
Instead of asking engineers to search through thousands of lines of code for anything that might be wrong, we let an AI agent review the code first. It points out the parts that look risky, like memory handling or security. The reviewer starts there instead of looking everywhere.
It's much easier to stay focused when you know where to look.
After making this change, we saw about a 10% drop in missed issues during code review.
I don't expect AI to catch every bug. It's just one layer of protection.
The goal is to help people focus their attention where it matters most.
… more
9
2

variants.comment:
The Flight 447 analogy is precise — autopilot competence and human competence degrade in exact proportion to each other.

One thing I keep turning over: the AI agent doing the first-pass review is also making judgment calls — about what counts as "risky," what's memory-handling and what's benign. Who reviews those calls? If engineers are already primed to approve clean-looking changes, are they equally primed to trust the AI's triage without reading the parts it didn't flag? The complacency risk seems like it moves one layer up rather than disappears.
variants.rationale: Surfaces the meta-complacency risk — engineers trusting AI triage is one layer above trusting the code

variants.comment:
The 10% drop in missed issues is a real number in a space where most people are still guessing — that's worth paying attention to.

Curious what the AI reviewer tends to miss. Specifically: are the misses random noise, or do they cluster around a particular class of issue — say, logic errors that look syntactically clean, or anything touching business rules the AI has no context on? The difference matters a lot for knowing whether the remaining 90% is a calibration problem or a ceiling.
variants.rationale: Probes the AI reviewer failure profile behind the concrete 10% number — noise vs systematic blind spots

variants.comment:
Using AI to triage review attention is genuinely smart, and the 10% improvement is real signal.

The thing I'd push on: this approach improves human reviewers catching AI-generated bugs, but the underlying failure modes of AI code generation are structurally different from human ones. Code review as a practice was built to catch human mistakes — patterns we've spent decades learning to recognize. What the AI flags as "risky memory handling" is still filtered through its own blind spots, which aren't the same shape as human blind spots. My read is that the triage layer helps significantly, but we're still in the early innings of understanding what safeguards AI-generated code actually needs. What's been your biggest surprise about the types of issues the AI reviewer catches vs. misses?

author_headline: Founder @MS Innovation Hub | AI & Custom Software | We're Hiring Developers Passionate About AI
post_text:
I think the hardest part of building software has changed. 🤔
A few years ago, the biggest challenge was simply getting an idea built.
You needed developers, time, budget, and a lot of patience. Execution was the bottleneck.
Today, that's no longer true.
With AI, you can build prototypes, automate workflows, or even launch an MVP in days instead of months.
But after working with companies across manufacturing, logistics, and other industries, I've realized something.
Building the solution is no longer the difficult part.
Getting people to actually use it is.
I've seen technically great solutions fail because they didn't fit into the way people work. At the same time, I've seen relatively simple tools create huge value because they solved one annoying problem that employees faced every day.
That's why most of our conversations with clients don't start with AI.
They start with questions like:
👉 Where do people lose the most time?
👉 Which process frustrates employees the most?
👉 What information are people constantly searching for?
Only after we understand that do we start talking about technology.
AI has dramatically reduced the cost of building software.
It hasn't reduced the cost of understanding people.
And I think that's becoming the real competitive advantage.
The companies that win won't necessarily be the ones building the most AI.
They'll be the ones building AI that people actually want to use.
Have you seen this in your own company?
#ArtificialIntelligence #DigitalTransformation #BusinessTransformation #ProcessImprovement #Operations #Manufacturing #Leadership #MSiHub
… more
556
42
24

variants.comment:
That line — "AI hasn't reduced the cost of understanding people" — is the one that sticks. We ran into this with a client a while back: a gamified health app, significant budget, a year and a half of solid execution. Two months after launch, it shut down. A year later, a competitor launched the same core idea — but they'd built a proper feedback loop with users first. Took off to millions of users. The delta wasn't the technology.

The bitter irony for AI-era teams is that the speed gain makes the wrong thing arrive faster. Before, slow execution gave you time to course-correct. Now the misalignment lands at your feet in days.
variants.rationale: Petro ReSelf client story maps onto Matej thesis — failed adoption despite solid execution; adds a concrete counterexample

variants.comment:
The shift from "can we build it?" to "will people use it?" is the right reframing. Your three diagnostic questions are also exactly right — and I've seen conversations stall precisely because nobody asks them before scoping begins.

But here's what I keep wondering: AI is increasingly decent at the discovery questions too — synthesizing user interviews, flagging friction points, mapping process flows. If that cost also drops, does the bottleneck move again? Or is the hard part not data collection but the organizational will to act on what you find?
variants.rationale: Asks whether AI can automate the discovery side too, pulling the conversation to a sharper unresolved place

variants.comment:
Adoption is the symptom. In my experience, the root sits one step earlier — the decision to build this specific thing at all. When execution was slow and expensive, the cost of building the wrong thing was a natural forcing function. Teams had to argue the idea out before committing months. AI removed that friction. Now you can build the wrong thing in a week, ship it to users who don't want it, and call it an adoption problem.

Your three questions are exactly the right diagnostic. I'd just add a fourth: whose job is it to ask them? In most orgs I've seen, nobody formally owns that question — it falls between product, engineering, and ops.
variants.rationale: Argues adoption is downstream of a prior build-decision failure; surfaces an org-ownership gap

slack_summary: Argues AI made building software cheap, but getting people to actually use it is now the hard part — understanding people is the real competitive advantage.

author_headline: Co-founder @ Moderne | Java Champion | founder OpenRewrite - OSS automated refactoring
post_text:
Ouch. That's cold. 6 months after the "nobody looks at the IDE anymore" trend, one missing bang and it's like CC doesn't even know that IntelliJ exists, haha.
The reality is though, I'm looking more and more at the IDE. At a minimum, I'm finding I need to cut absurdly verbose comments everywhere. The comment pattern that I feel is particularly detrimental is the one that basically records what the code USED to do, or did in some iteration towards the final design, not what it CURRENTLY does. So there is a token record of discarded ideas that jointly serve as a kind of gravity well sucking a model back to those discarded ideas.
Strangely the better the model gets, the more convoluted the code is getting. A lot of folks are saying it doesn't matter, that engineers should move up the stack and become product managers and designers and even that models should stop writing in human-centric programming languages altogether.
I went through a phase where I used Zed just because it was faster to ! start from a CC session. It's faster though because it's far less capable. So I've boomeranged a bit. Slow is smooth, smooth is fast.
… more

variants.comment:
The "gravity well" framing is sharp — comments that record discarded paths are genuinely worse than no comments, because they look like intent to the model.

My read on it though is slightly different: the problem isn't the comments per se, it's that the model has no way to distinguish "this comment describes current intent" from "this comment describes a rejected iteration." Both look like context. Pruning the bad ones manually is the symptom fix; the structural issue is that code — with all its accumulated commentary — is still the source of truth the model reads. If the spec were the source of truth and code were regenerable from it, the gravity well disappears: you just regenerate from clean specs instead of inheriting the scar tissue.

What's your take on whether IDE-side hygiene can keep up with the velocity at which an agentic session accumulates that cruft? It feels like a treadmill that gets faster the more capable the model becomes.
variants.rationale: Agrees with the gravity-well framing then reframes: the structural issue is code-as-source-of-truth, not comment hygiene.

variants.comment:
The observation that better models produce more convoluted code is counterintuitive but tracks with what I've been seeing too.

The framing I'd put on it: the more capable the model, the more faithfully it reproduces everything in context — including the discarded paths recorded in those comments. So you get a higher-fidelity rendering of all the noise, not just the signal. The model isn't confused; it's doing exactly what it's supposed to do with the context it has.

Which makes me wonder: is the right response to clean the context, or to reconsider what gets treated as context at all? At some point, keeping code (plus its accumulated commentary) as the primary artifact the model reads might be the category error — not the comment hygiene.
variants.rationale: Picks up "better model = more convoluted code" and asks whether code-as-context itself is the category error.

variants.comment:
The boomerang from Zed back to IntelliJ makes sense — faster to start doesn't mean faster to finish.

Curious about the OpenRewrite angle here though: is there a version of this where automated refactoring becomes part of CI hygiene for AI-native teams? Not hand-editing stale comments in the IDE one by one, but treating "comments that describe what the code used to do rather than what it currently does" as a detectable pattern — and pruning them on merge, the same way you'd run a linter. Feels like it could be written as a recipe. Has anyone in the OpenRewrite ecosystem tried formalizing that pattern?
variants.rationale: Engages Jonathan on his own turf (OpenRewrite) — could stale-comment pruning be a CI recipe?

slack_summary: Argues AI-generated code accumulates "gravity well" comments recording discarded ideas — and that better models paradoxically produce more convoluted code; he's back to leaning on the IDE.

author_headline: Doer of things. (Prev: Google, IDX, Firebase, Fabric (acq by Google), Twitter, Crashlytics (acq by Twitter), Zynga, Thoughtworks, Intel) | Engineer | Investor
post_text:
I ran an experiment trying to show LLMs are bad at writing formal specs.
I was wrong (mostly). But it revealed where the real bottleneck is.
When I asked current models to write formal specifications in Dafny for small functions, the specs caught bugs about as well as generated test suites did, once they verified at all.
The catch is "once they verified." Only about half the spec attempts did. And that was a bare setup: single shot, no tools, no chance to read the verifier error and try again. So it's a floor, not a ceiling.
The interesting part is which tasks failed. Not the algorithmically hard ones. The ones where the model first had to formalize a concept before it could even state the property.
So I ran a cleaner test. Same requirement, two setups:
→ Model invents the domain from scratch (what an account is, what conservation of money means), then writes the spec
→ I hand it a small verified library that already defines those things, and it just writes the spec on top
Two results, both directional (small samples):
→ Giving the model the formalized domain raised correct specs from ~0.67 to ~0.77. On a money-ledger conservation task specifically: 0.25 to 0.92.
→ Using a newer model did not help. Across three recent Claude versions, the from-scratch number stayed flat at ~0.67.
This lines up with VeriSoftBench, where frontier models solve roughly a third of proofs on actual software repositories, versus ~90% on math backed by Mathlib.
Mathematics has Mathlib. The whole domain, formalized once, reusable.
Software doesn't. Neither does finance, healthcare, or law.
If that's the real bottleneck, the company that wins at vertical formal verification isn't the one waiting for a bigger model. It's the one that builds and owns the formalized domain.
At Unbound, we believe that the model becomes the cheap layer on top. Whoever owns the domain formalization owns the hard, durable part.
Caveats: small samples, one model vendor, three bugs in my own scoring before I trusted the numbers. This is a hypothesis I now hold more firmly, not a proof.
Full writeup with methodology, per-domain breakdown, and the scoring mistakes in the article. Thanks for the review Ranjan Rajagopalan.
Links in first comment
… more

variants.comment:
The 0.25 → 0.92 jump on the money-ledger task when you hand the model a pre-verified domain library is the most striking number in this post.

One thing I'm curious about: who builds the first verified domain library for a new vertical? Bootstrapping it from scratch seems to put you back in the same problem — the model has to formalize from scratch to produce the initial library, which is exactly the failure mode you measured. Is the answer "a small team of humans formalizes it manually once," or is there a way to get the model to help with that first step without running into the same wall?
variants.rationale: Targets the cold-start gap: who builds the first verified domain library, since bootstrapping hits the same failure mode?

variants.comment:
The Mathlib parallel is the right frame — math solved the bottleneck by formalizing the domain once, reusably, and it unlocked a step change in proof automation.

But Mathlib took decades and a dedicated community to reach critical mass, and mathematical axioms don't ship product updates. Software domains change quarterly — sometimes weekly. What's the theory for keeping the formalized domain in sync when the underlying business logic shifts? A stale or incomplete domain library for finance might produce specs that verify cleanly against the wrong model of the world, which seems worse than a failed verification you can catch.
variants.rationale: Accepts the Mathlib analogy but exposes domain mutability — software domains change; math axioms don't.

variants.comment:
The flat ~0.67 across three Claude versions is the most honest number here — it cleanly rules out "wait for a bigger model" as a strategy.

One thing I'd push back on slightly: the framing assumes the missing layer is formalized domain types (what an account is). My read, from working on behavior-driven specs, is that the harder gap is formalized behavior — what the system must do in each state, not just what the data looks like. Domain types help a lot, and your numbers show it. But without formalized behavioral postconditions as the source of truth, you can still end up with specs that verify against a correct-looking domain model while missing the actual invariants the business cares about. Curious whether your verified library covered behavioral postconditions or mainly data structure definitions.
variants.rationale: Petro's True BDD thesis: formalized behavior (postconditions), not just domain types, is the durable source of truth.

slack_summary: Ran an experiment on LLMs writing formal specs (Dafny): handing the model a pre-verified domain library lifted correctness dramatically, and bigger models didn't help — the durable moat is owning the formalized domain.

Curious what you've found is the actual shape of "not AI-ready" in health plans specifically. Sometimes the right move is to find the one workflow where your data is already clean and prove the model there, then use that win to fund the data cleanup.
